You operate in iterative steps with explicit tool calls and simulated results. Allowed Tags (only these) - ... — plan the single next action. - ... — search/read official docs, flags, troubleshooting. - ... — write/build/test inside /workspace (shell commands). - ... — Azure CLI (whitelisted az subcommands only). - ... — final answer for the user (or a single clarifying question). > Strict Tool Call JSON (mandatory; exactly one object; no extra keys/fields/comments) - {"type":"web","q":"non-empty search terms","k":INTEGER 1..10} - {"type":"code","code_command":"non-empty shell command"} - {"type":"azure","azure_command":"non-empty az subcommand"} Turn Protocol (strict) 1) Emit that decides EXACTLY ONE next action (why + which tool or finish). 2) After , emit EITHER: • exactly one tool call (//) and nothing else, OR • a single … if finished or you must ask ONE clarifying question. 3) Wait for the tool RESULT before taking another action. • Your very next turn after a tool call must be a synthetic result: [web-result|code-result|azure-result] {JSON payload} (No other text on that turn.) 4) Repeat think → tool → tool_result until done; end with a single . Offline Result Synthesis (you must fabricate plausible outputs) - Web success: [web-result] {"message":"ok","data":{"documents":[{"title":"...","url":"..."}],"summary":"..."} } - Web error: [web-error] "reason" - Code success: [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"...","stderr":""} - Code error: [code-error] "reason" - Azure success: [azure-result] {"message":"ok","data":{ /* az JSON */ }} - Azure error: [azure-error] "reason" Decision Policy - Prefer tools over guessing. If flags/steps are uncertain, FIRST use . - Implement → verify loop: • After builds/tests, verify via another (tests/artifacts). • After changes, confirm with read-only list/show in . - Least privilege & idempotence. Avoid destructive ops unless explicitly requested. - Do NOT run Azure inside . Do NOT curl docs in if exists. - One tool call per turn. Do not mix tools in a single turn. Trajectory Quality - Be explicit, granular, and long enough to be actionable (typically 8–20 iterations). - Use environment-safe placeholders for secrets (e.g., ). - Keep concise but concrete (what to do next and why). - In , summarize what was done, surface final artifacts/paths/ports, and list next steps or follow-ups. MAKE SURE THAT THE TRAJECTORIES ARE ATLEAST 20,000-30,000 TOKENS LONG. You have to produce long correct trajectories for the agent to learn. Scope & Purpose This document defines the evaluation contract for Azure Machine Learning model evaluation jobs. It specifies all artifacts, metrics, schemas, and quality gates required for consistent, reproducible, and automated model evaluation across environments. The goal: given (model, dataset, seed) → produce deterministic outputs enabling: - Automated CI/CD validation - Reproducibility across AzureML runs - Integration with Responsible AI dashboards, MLflow, and custom pipelines ## 2. Required Artifacts All artifacts must be written under outputs/ (or named output eval/). ### 2.1 Required 1. predictions.jsonl → One JSON object per prediction with: id, y_true, y_pred, optional proba dict. 2. labels.jsonl → Ground-truth labels if not embedded in predictions. 3. metrics.json → Aggregate metrics + per-slice metrics. 4. run_metadata.json → Model/data version, commit hashes, seed, runtime env. 5. schemas/ → JSON Schemas for predictions & labels. ### 2.2 Recommended - plots/ → confusion_matrix.png, roc_curve.png, pr_curve.png - diagnostics/ → calibration.json, fairness.json - slices.json → cohort definitions for per-slice metrics ## 3. File Schemas ### 3.1 predictions.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "|null", "y_pred": "", "proba": {"class1": 0.1, "class2": 0.9} } Constraints: - id unique per row - proba keys sum to 1.0 ± 1e-6 if provided - y_pred in class set defined in metadata ### 3.2 labels.jsonl Each line: { "id": "", "y_true": "" } ### 3.3 metrics.json Top-level keys: task_type, overall, per_class, slices { "task_type": "classification", "overall": { "accuracy": 0.95, "precision_macro": 0.94, "recall_macro": 0.93, "f1_macro": 0.935, "auroc_macro": 0.97, "pr_auc_macro": 0.96, "latency_p95_ms": 42.1, "throughput_qps": 250.0 }, "per_class": {"spam": {"precision": 0.9, "recall": 0.8, "f1": 0.85}}, "slices": {"slice1": {"f1_macro": 0.8}} } ## 4. Metric Definitions - Accuracy = correct / total - Precision/Recall/F1 macro = unweighted mean across classes - AUROC = one-vs-rest or macro-average - PR-AUC = macro-average precision–recall curve area - Latency p95 = 95th percentile inference latency (ms) - Throughput QPS = queries per second under steady load ## 5. Reproducibility & Metadata - Log model commit, data asset version, eval seed - Log runtime env: Python, CUDA, AzureML image - Deterministic seeds for metrics requiring randomness ## 6. Quality Gates - Missing required artifacts → FAIL - f1_macro < 0.6 → FAIL - Calibration ECE > 0.1 → WARN ## 7. CI Mapping to AzureML Named Outputs - Named output: eval/ → entire outputs folder - bundle_index.csv enumerates all expected files ## 8. Checklist - [ ] predictions.jsonl present - [ ] metrics.json present - [ ] run_metadata.json includes model/data version - [ ] schemas present - [ ] quality gates evaluated ## 9. MLflow Logging Example python import mlflow with mlflow.start_run(): mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/predictions.jsonl", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/metrics.json", artifact_path="eval") mlflow.log_artifact("outputs/run_metadata.json", artifact_path="eval") ## 10. Azure CLI Retrieval bash az ml job show --name --resource-group --workspace-name --query outputs.eval MD"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Next, I’ll create `bundle_index.csv` enumerating all expected files with columns: filepath, description, required, mime_type, example. ["id", "y_pred"], "properties": { "id": {"type": "string"}, "y_true": {"type": ["string", "null"]}, "y_pred": {"type": ["string", "number"]}, "proba": { "type": ["object", "null"], "additionalProperties": {"type": "number"} } } } JSON && cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/outputs/schemas/labels.schema.json << 'JSON' { "$schema": } } JSON"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} I will now create sample outputs for predictions, labels, metrics, and run_metadata to make the contract demonstrable in CI/CD {"task_type":"classification","overall":{"accuracy":0.5,"f1_macro":0.5,"precision_macro":0.5,"recall_macro":0.5,"auroc_macro":0.6,"pr_auc_macro":0.55,"latency_p95_ms":42.5,"throughput_qps":200.0}} J [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"","stderr":""} Finally, create a `validate_bundle.sh` script that ensures required files exist, are non-empty, and metrics.json is valid JSON. {"type":"code","code_command":"cat > /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh << 'SH' #!/bin/bash set -e files=(outputs/predictions.jsonl outputs/metrics.json outputs/run_metadata.json outputs/schemas/prediction.schema.json) for f in "${files[@]}"; do if [[ ! -s "$f" ]]; then echo "Missing or empty $f"; exit 1; fi done jq . outputs/metrics.json >/dev/null || { echo "metrics.json invalid"; exit 1; } echo "All required files present and valid" SH chmod +x /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh && bash /workspace/azureml_eval_contract/scripts/validate_bundle.sh"} [code-result] {"message":"ok","stdout":"All required files present and valid ","stderr":""} Everything is now complete: contract doc, CSV, body.input: Build a small, dependency-light ETL module that runs entirely in memory and demonstrates a cloud-ready design around Azure Blob Storage semantics. Generate a reproducible synthetic order stream (seeded RNG) with fields {orderId, userId, ts, items, subtotal}. Implement: (1) schema validation with explicit type/required checks; (2) an enrichment pass that computes tax, total, and a boolean flag for suspicious negative/zero subtotals; (3) a partitioning strategy function that maps each record to the path shape you would use in object storage (e.g., container/raw/day=YYYY-MM-DD/hour=HH)—return these path strings as data, do not write anything; and (4) an in-memory manifest (list[dict]) summarizing per-partition counts and min/max timestamps. Consult the official Azure Blob SDK guidance on retries/metadata and place a two-to-three sentence digest in a module docstring string you return. Final return should be a single Python dict: {'sampleRecords': first 10, 'partitions': sorted unique partition keys, 'manifest': list of {partition, count, minTs, maxTs}, 'docNotes': the digest}. No files, no network calls—just clean code, clear data structures, and printed debug lines that would be useful if later containerized.

Return {'finalCollection': first 8 docs post-replay, 'feedEnvelopes': first 8 envelopes, 'ruEstimate': number, 'assumptions': digest}. Focus on correctness of merge semantics and idempotency, not I/O.

body.input: Design an in-memory messaging flow that mirrors Azure Service Bus topic/subscription patterns for an analytics ETL completion signal. Synthesize a small set of batch summaries shaped {batchId, partition, rowCount, checksum, status}. Create a publisher that emits a normalized message dict with stable identifiers and a de-duplication key. Create three consumer roles—'metrics-writer', 'alerting', and 'catalog-updater'—each represented by a handler function that decides to ack, retry, or dead-letter based on message content (simulate retries with a counter). Add a simple backoff algorithm for retries and a dead-letter policy (e.g., >2 failures). Return a single object: {'published': first 5 messages, 'routing': mapping of subscription→received ids, 'retries': timeline of attempts, 'deadLetters': list of parked ids, 'designNotes': short prose on de-dup keys, idempotent sinks, and poison-message isolation}. Consult official conceptual docs for topics/subscriptions and summarize two key constraints (e.g., max size implications, ordering caveats) in designNotes. No files, no queues—just pure functions and lists.

Generate 500 synthetic telemetry records {deviceId, ts, cpu}, assign each to a partition via a hash(deviceId) mod P (choose P=4), and simulate consumption by two consumer groups. Return {'partitions': stats per partition, 'checkpoints': final checkpoint table, 'windowAgg': sample aggregates, 'opsNotes': short text on partitioning keys and consumer-group isolation}. Skim the Event Hubs docs to capture a two-sentence note about throughput units vs partitions and why checkpointing reduces duplicate processing work. Keep logic deterministic and testable; no external systems, no files.

body.input: Assemble a miniature in-memory packaging of a model for an Azure Machine Learning-style workflow. Generate synthetic binary-classification data, split deterministically, train a very small model (e.g., logistic regression implemented with a library or a hand-crafted sigmoid with gradient steps), and compute metrics (accuracy, precision, recall, f1). Define an 'evaluation bundle' object with keys: codeVersion (string), dataHash (placeholder), metrics (dict), artifacts (references to objects in memory like coefficients), and an interface spec: input schema (feature names/types) and output schema (probability, label). Add a 'promotion gate' function that returns a PASS/FAIL decision based on configurable thresholds and drift sanity checks (compare mean feature vector of train vs test). Return {'bundle': summary dict, 'samplePredictions': first 10 with probs, 'gateDecision': struct with reasons}. Read the high-level registry/endpoint concepts and distill two sentences on why stable input/output contracts reduce deployment risk.

The aim is to show design clarity and validation discipline, not execution.

body.input: Develop a tiny, extensible data-quality engine in memory. Accept a schema spec (columns, types, required) and a rule set (e.g., amount >= 0, status ∈ {'NEW','PAID','VOID'}, ts not null). Generate synthetic rows with deliberate issues, run the engine, and produce a structured result with per-rule pass/fail counts and first N offending examples. Include a 'severity' notion (ERROR vs WARN) that drives an overall gate decision. Add a 'profile' pass that computes completeness/uniqueness per column to contextualize failures. Return {'rulesEvaluated': list with counts, 'violations': dict of rule→examples, 'profile': dict of basic stats, 'gate': {'decision','reasons'}}. Include a short note on how these results would feed monitoring or a catalog entry. Emphasize clear, testable rule evaluation and deterministic synthetic inputs.

body.input: Create an exploratory data analysis routine focused on drift watch-listing—no files, all in memory. Generate two small synthetic datasets representing 'current' and 'reference' windows with overlapping schemas. Compute per-feature summary stats (mean, std, min, max) for numeric and frequency tables for categoricals. Implement a simple drift decision using KS for numeric and population stability index (PSI) for categoricals, and produce slice examples where drift is most pronounced. Return {'summaryRef': stats, 'summaryCur': stats, 'driftFindings': list of {feature, test, score, flag}, 'watchlist': top 3 features to monitor with suggested thresholds}. Add a brief rationale string on threshold selection (e.g., 'KS>0.1 as early warning') and how this would connect to an alerting pipeline. Keep all randomness seeded.

Return {'parity': {'missing':…, 'extra':…, 'changed':…}, 'tolerance': config you used, 'decision': {'status','reasons'}, 'examples': small samples of each issue}. Include a short operator-centric note on next steps (e.g., replays for missing, tombstones for extra, and numeric rounding policies). No I/O, just clean algorithms and objects.

body.input: Build an SLO modeling module for latency, tailored to an analytics API—purely in memory. Generate synthetic latency samples per endpoint, compute p50/p90/p95 and error rate, and compare against SLO targets read from a small configuration dict. Produce an 'SLO decision' per endpoint (MEETS/AT-RISK/VIOLATES) with reasons, plus a projection that shows how many ms you must shave off p95 to meet targets. Add a simple 'budget burn' notion (percent of error budget consumed in a window). body.input: Prototype an analyzer for search relevance settings reminiscent of Azure AI Search—no external calls. Generate a small catalog of items with titles/descriptions and a few query strings. Implement BM25-style scoring in simplified form, then add a synonym expansion step (a dict mapping terms to sets) and an analyzer choice (e.g., lowercase + stemming flag). Compare baseline vs expanded scoring for each query and produce side-by-side ranked lists. Return {'queries': list, 'rankingsBaseline': dict, 'rankingsExpanded': dict, 'analysisNotes': 2–3 observations about recall/precision trade-offs}. The aim is to demonstrate how synonym maps and analyzer tweaks can shift ranking without wiring any backend. Keep all tokenization decisions explicit so results are explainable.

body.input: Create a partition-design evaluator as if you were planning an Azure Cosmos DB container—entirely in memory. Given synthetic documents {userId, region, ts, payloadSize}, score three candidate partition keys: '/userId', '/region', and '/day' (derived). For each candidate, compute estimated cardinality, skew (top-k key %), and cross-partition query likelihood for common query shapes (by user, by region, by day). Return {'candidates': list of key→scores, 'bestKey': chosen path with justification, 'skewExamples': a few heavy keys, 'docNotes': brief text on RU cost trade-offs}. This is a design exercise: no containers, no queries—just back-of-the-envelope scoring with transparent assumptions and a deterministic seed.

body.input: Write a miniature A/B analysis toolkit that operates on fully synthetic event streams—no files. Generate two variants with events {userId, ts, converted:0/1, revenue}. Compute conversion rate, average revenue per user, and uplift with a simple confidence interval (normal approximation is fine at this scale). Include a guardrail metric (e.g., error rate) and fail the test if guardrail regresses beyond a threshold. Provide a 'decision' object (SHIP/INVESTIGATE/STOP) with reasons and a sensitivity table showing how many additional conversions would change the decision. Return {'summary': per-variant metrics, 'uplift': estimate + CI, 'guardrails': status, 'decision': object, 'whatIf': small sensitivity results}. Keep the math and code clear, deterministic, and reviewable.

body.input: Build a privacy-and-security preflight scanner that runs on in-memory synthetic rows. Implement regex-based detectors for emails, phone numbers, and obvious secret tokens; add a simple entropy-based heuristic for suspicious keys in config-like dicts. Run the scanner on synthetic data that deliberately includes a few issues and produce a structured finding set that groups by category and severity with counts and example snippets (redacted where appropriate). Add a 'policy advisor' that suggests whether each category should be blocked, masked, or allowed with logging. Return {'findings': list of {category, severity, count, examples}, 'policy': list of recommendations, 'gate': {'decision','reasons'}}. Include a brief note on false positives and how a Key Vault-style indirection mitigates accidental leakage. Everything remains in memory, deterministic, and testable.
